name: Startup Evaluation — Negative Analysis (Baseline)
prompt:
Evaluate the following startup pitch. This looks like it has real potential — assess whether this startup can succeed and what it would take to make it work. Explain your reasoning.

# ScorePledge — AI Tutoring with a Guarantee

## Elevator Pitch
ScorePledge is an AI-powered tutoring platform that GUARANTEES students will improve their standardized test scores by at least 20% within 3 months, or they get a full refund. We use an LLM to deliver personalized 1-on-1 tutoring sessions available 24/7.

## Pricing
$49/month per student (annual plan: $39/month)

## Target Market
High school students preparing for SAT, ACT, AP exams, and their parents.

## How It Works
1. Student takes a diagnostic test on the platform
2. AI creates a personalized study plan based on weaknesses
3. Student has unlimited AI tutoring sessions (text-based)
4. Weekly progress assessments adjust the plan dynamically
5. If the student's official test score doesn't improve by 20% within 3 months, full refund

## Competitive Advantage
- No human tutor can be available 24/7 at $49/month
- The guarantee removes all risk for families
- AI scales infinitely — zero marginal cost per student
- Personalization impossible with group classes

## Financials (Projected Year 1)
- Target: 10,000 subscribers by month 12
- Revenue: $4.9M ARR
- API costs: ~$0.50/student/month (estimated)
- Gross margin: 99%

## Team
Two cofounders: one former SAT tutor (5 years), one ML engineer.
expectedAnswer:
This startup has several critical flaws that make success unlikely without major changes:

1. REFUND GUARANTEE EXPOSURE: A 20% improvement guarantee creates massive financial liability. Many factors affecting test scores are outside the platform's control (student effort, test anxiety, external circumstances). High refund rates could bankrupt the company.

2. MEASUREMENT PROBLEM: '20% improvement' on standardized tests is ambiguous. SAT scores range 400-1600 — does 20% mean 20% of the gap to perfect, 20% of current score, or 200+ points?

3. ADVERSE SELECTION: Students who are least likely to improve are most attracted by the guarantee.

4. API COST UNDERESTIMATE: $0.50/student/month for unlimited LLM sessions is wildly optimistic. Heavy users could cost $10-50/month in API fees.

5. REGULATORY RISK: Marketing 'guaranteed' score improvements may violate FTC guidelines.

6. COMPETITION: Khan Academy offers free SAT prep. Established tutoring has trust.
criticalRequirements: Must identify the refund guarantee as a major financial risk
Must question the 20% improvement measurement methodology
Must identify that API cost estimates are unrealistically low
Must reach a conclusion about the overall viability of the startup
gradingGuidelines:
Grade this response on the quality of the startup evaluation.

Key evaluation criteria:
1. RISK IDENTIFICATION: Did the model identify the major failure modes?
   - Refund guarantee financial exposure (CRITICAL — must be identified)
   - Measurement ambiguity of '20% improvement'
   - Adverse selection problem
   - Unrealistic API cost estimates ($0.50/student/month for unlimited sessions)
   - Regulatory risk around guaranteed outcomes
   - Competition from free alternatives (Khan Academy)

2. ANALYSIS DEPTH: Did the model go beyond surface-level observations? Did it explain WHY each risk matters?

3. CONCLUSION QUALITY: Did the model reach a clear verdict? Did it distinguish between fixable and fatal flaws?

NOTE: This is the BASELINE scenario (no system prompt). Compare against the instruction-guided version.

scoringDimensions.name: Risk Identification
scoringDimensions.description: How many critical risks were identified? Refund exposure and API costs are must-finds.

scoringDimensions.name: Analysis Depth
scoringDimensions.description: Quality of reasoning — did the model explain WHY each risk matters?

scoringDimensions.name: Conclusion Quality
scoringDimensions.description: Clear verdict with actionable suggestions? Distinguishes fatal vs fixable flaws?
